text: [It's a leg lace.]( This is not like a rear naked choke or [omoplata]( which are intended to inflict so much pain or restrict circulation such that your opponent concedes the match. It's a turn that rolls your opponent, scoring points for exposure (exposing opponent's back to the mat).
label: analysis

text: More teams should go for 2 instead of OT. OT is about a 50/50 shot at winning. Going for 2 is about a 55% conversion rate. Going for 1 *seems* safer because it extends the game. But going for 2 is actually more successful in terms of winning. The reason more teams don't do so can be seen in your comment: >not converting leads to an automatic loss People focus on not coverting leading to an automatic loss instead of focusing on converting leading to an automatic win.
label: analysis

text: Man, I tried to join the Air Force after high school, but they made it pretty clear to me I needed to drop any dream of being a pilot or PJ. I was a vocational drafting/mechanical engineering and design student and that was what I'd be doing in the Air Force because it was too rare a skill for a young person to have not to take advantage of. A Marines recruiter in the office next door snickered "Another one for the Chair Force!" as he overheard the conversation. So I ended up backing out. I didn't really care if I had a non-combat MOS, but I honestly didn't even really like engineering. The thought of doing that instead of something more challenging was a real let down. Like 4 of my buddies did end up enlisting, so I was sad I didn't go with them, but they let me know I didn't miss much haha.
label: reaction

text: Right. And I bet you'd also bet it's harder to do on foot than on a bike. And you'd be right that the top speed on foot is less than the top speed on ice is less than the top speed on bike. But the 100m times (an NHL rink is 61m; I'm not figuring out his exact distance) aren't all that different as it's such a short distance to use your legs to accelerate in. The top speed difference on skates or bike comes from the fact that you don't lose your momentum as quickly once you've got it, so you can add more to it. But you need distance in which to do that.
label: analysis

text: Yes and if he did hit those speeds, he may be easier to dodge, as long as you have enough time to predict where he's going and you have enough speed. It's easy to hit someone when they're going slow, but if you're going too fast, you can't be as shifty. Plus he would get a charging penalty. McDavid is shifty enough at the right speed, and has hands that backs the defenseman up, rather than them trying to step on him.

text: The thing I don't like about this drill is most people have a strong side and a weak side for turning. And if you're right-handed as most people are (not necessarily right shooting), you usually turn left better. So McDavid has an advantage in this drill, assuming they're both right-handed. They should make them take one corner one way and the other corner the other way. You can see it in how Mackinnon doesn't do cross-overs in the turn. I bet if he were turning to his left he could get some cross-overs in.
